Abstract   This study aims to address the question: Why conflict persist in Mali despite the fact that many strategy of conflict resolution was defined and developed by the United Nations and other nations.  This work will present an analysis of the evolution of peacekeeping activities, as defined by several figures, such as the former UN secretary general Ko f fi Annan  who defined  peacekeepin g operations as “ a preventive function that intends to avert the outb reak or recurrence of conflict and  their preventive role has been particularly clear where they have been deployed before the beginning of an armed inter nal or international conflict” . We will proceed by  presenting the evolution of peacekeeping operations through time. In a critical analysis, it stresses the missions’ limitations. With unfit and unclear mandates in Africa , the  main goal of reaching an agreement is insufficient in today’s world thus this study shows that an agreement or a mission  will not erase years of resentment , nor w ill it lead people to a peace after years of war. In line to resolutions and  conclusions, this study proposes John Paul Lederach’s theory on Conflict Transformation as an alternative strategy to rethink peacekeeping operations. Highlighting the importance of relationships to achieve reconciliation, it emphasizes the central role individuals must play to achieve peace.  Through the example of the conflict in Mali , we come to address the fact that  there is the need to rethink peace operations as a whole because  of  conflict  intensifications . Peacekeeping progressed  rapidly from  what was at first  a  traditional, primarily military  model of observing cease-fires and enforced separatio n after inter-state wars, to integrate  an elaborated  model where many elements, military and civilian, work together to maintain peace in the dangerous aftermath of violent conflict.  Relevance  of the study  and thesis proposal After the  Second World  War,  different  countries  decided  to join their efforts with the aim of finding  a solution to prevent another war outbreak and  maintain peace . They needed an institution above nations that have a measure of extreme security there by setting up an institution named United Nations, which had the main purposes;   To keep peace throughout the world , t o develop friendly relations among nations  and t o help nations work together to improve the lives of poor people, to conquer hunger, disease and illiteracy, and to encourage respect for each other’s rights and freedoms .  The UN therefore is  a center for harmonizing the actions of nations to  achieve these goals and through this , it has set up a certain number of instruments to prevent wars from re-emerging.  UN peacekeeping activities are the main international response for  peace building , and peace enforcement efforts within stat es experiencing instability and conflict .  Peacekeeping  can be seen   as  “ the essential component for decreasing number of severe armed conflicts worldwide since the 1990s ” ,  but the  presence of a peacekeeping operation has been argued to prolong the post-conflict peace period ,  or  decrease the amount of battle-related deaths in a n ongoing conflict .  The  big   debate on its  success is often argued  to relay on the features  of the peacekeeping mission  i.e.   stronger  mandates, together with larger budgets and more p ersonnel are aspects that have been  judged to impact  Peacekeeping  activities’ successfulness  positively . However, other factors, that may or m ay not affect the  success  of  th ese activities sometimes still remain  overlooked.    This is  precisely the idea of peacekeep ing activities that we intend  to analyze  and understand  its  s uccessful ness  in achieving lon g-lasting peace or rather  present an alternative approach  in other to respond to the problem posed by this thesis .  We will propose  not only to study the theoretical approaches and background definitions and ideas on peacekeep ing operations but also  their evolution and efficacy over time.  In general, l ack of coordination between UN departments and agencies working in the field may hamper the pro spects of achieving a consensus as it can be easily   manipulated by the  opposite  parties to get  each  a  deal in the negotiations  that suits each interest .  A  possible response to this question would be to rethink the current peacekeeping strategies: different  problems requires different solutions thus different  conflicts and contexts require different approaches and strategies .   R ather than to stick to  think that  a specific  path will always work best in every situation ,  it’s  good to try new approaches. May be for example a  new  approach of seeing the conflict or bringing  groups together than concentrating on the authorities. The Problem   Despite the resources and treaties established to prevent con flict worldwide, we can still find conflict persistence   particularly in Africa which brings us to  question the usefulness of  Peacekeeping interventions .  The case of Africa nowadays, 54 African countries are members of the UN and some have come to realize that despite the interventions of the UN there is not a big change and on the contrary in  some cases it is getting worse. We should keep in mind that  this research is more focused on understanding why conflict persist than trying to understand if peacekeeping activities are successful in the long term or not. In many cases, conflicts frizzle out, armed conflicts end, but the underlying reasons and contradictions remain, thus increasing the  probability to break out again .   The case of Mali  is very interesting  as  it is one of the most conflictual zone in Africa  and  Malians  are continuously  questioning the UN commitment and France engagement especially.  We can perceive their  me contentment  through one of their current decision of driving out the French forces; with the reason that they question their intervention in  the conflict on the territory.  They  also recently  embraced the Russians forces  with a lot of ambition and motivations. In addition to this, the main motivation and relevance is found in its contribution to the overall discussion is  determining how  successful and effective Peacekeeping activities really are and reveal the persistence of conflict despite  the efforts  to deescalate it. Even though a large scope of literature does agree that peacekeeping is successful in establishing and keeping peace, there is no consensus on the fact that lasting peace comes back. The findings made here hope to shed light on the dynamics which hinders peace from deescalating in Mali. The roadmap of the paper is as follows: the following section will situate this thesis within previous research of peacekeeping effectiveness. It will then draw from previous studies to pres ent its theoretical argument. It will also illustrate the  discrete-time  data on  evolution of  conflicts  throughout years  used for testing the hypotheses  and observing what is lacking . Upon th is, the results will be  discussed. As the main hypothesis could not be support ed , potential caveats in the research design and methods together with alternative explanations are discussed. The thesis  will end  in a conclusive discussion of the study’s implications and some reflections for future research of the relationship between peacekeeping operation success  and the measures employed in calming conflicts which will be useful for other nations. On a peace that remains precarious, it is necessary to question these militias created by the UN to maintain peace and also the contribution of other forces as P eacekeeping can clearly articulate its importance at the strategic level: it contributes to global peace and security, promotes human rights and supports national authorities in protecting civilians; it can create space for political solutions to be found, and can help peace to take root.  The key research question to be answered is :   Why conflicts still persist despite efforts made by peacekeeping activities to  deescalate; using  Mali  as a case study. HYPOTHESIS We can hypothesize on the fact that maybe  T he UN has not been able to maintain peace because the organization is failing  in integrating its missions ;     In Vallings ’  t hesis ,  he   states  that because  of   some  politica l and administrative reasons,  integrated missions  are  under-perform ed   and also  limited  organizational  lear ning within the UN system also  prevents the improvement of integrated mission performance.  The fact that the staff in the integrated missions  tries to apply what they studied i.e. operational lessons en able s   them  to  make small-scale changes but makes  them to  in most cases neglect wider or  more fundamental change in how the UN carries out integra ted missions. P robably , the problem  lies in the role of UN member state interests overriding concern for quality of integrated mission performance, as well as in the administrative procedu r es of UN functioning. The UN could  be considered  therefore limited institutionally as it is prevented from implementing the lessons of its integrated mission experience. The  UN fails in mediating  and became comfortable and  contempt  with some of it successes  thereby  neglecting some areas ; Between  1987 and 1991, the United Nations mediated a series of agreements that helped end fighting between Iran and Iraq, led to the withdrawal of Soviet forces from Afghani stan and also  ended El Salvador's chronic civil war. All these series of success encouraged the view that it was the Cold War that had prevented the United Nations fr om being an effective mediator.  L ooking at the Congo  episode,  it further undermined the credi bility of the United Nations as the  U.N. has consist ently failed to expose, prevent, and punish  criminal behavior s of its own personnel . One of  the zones which was to be clarified was  the  a buse by U.N. personnel in some  U.N. peacekeeping operations   such as in;  Kosovo, Sierra Leone, Liberia, and Burundi   as  there was to be  independent investigations .  Also, t he U.N.  c ould lift diplomatic immunity for its own staff accused of criminal acts in the Congo, opening the way for prosecution.  Some nations may take advantage of the fact that the conflict continues; an example  can be seen with the ongoing crisis in Cameroon. Thousands have fled the English-speaking areas of the country because of violence and unrest, and many are calling for an independent state. The question in every  one’s  mind is to know what the government has done to alleviate this situation, and does it go far enough? Because it has been for years that the minority English-speaking community in Cameroon has been expressing its disillusionment with the reunification process that brought the English and the French-speaking parts of the country together in 1961.They complain about  marginalization  and under- representation in the country who is seen as administratively  Fran cophone-dominated. So the  Pro-Anglophone movements  took  advantage of the strikes to demand a return to a federal system of administration that was abolished in 1972 while others have been pushing for the establishment of an independent state, separate from the majority French-speaking areas.  “ Sometimes  some parties take advantage of instability to bring back stability ” .           Peacekeeping is considered one of the global community’s most effective tools for sustaining or restoring peace, but we can struggle with some fundamental questions  as objectives to be answered : Objectives Is  peace merely the absence of war?  Or it is much more ? What benefit do mission-supported negotiations by the French forces  in Mali bring to, say, a  shepherd  in the country? Has  the number of fatalities changed over the past decades?  If yes, does it means the operations are successful? If no, does it means operations are ineffective? When peace fails, is it because the mission failed, or is there more to the story? Is peacekeeping missions conducted in Mali  today provide enough guarantee to hinder conflict escalation ?   LITERATURE REVIEW Scholars  dif fers on how to measure the effectiveness of Peacekeeping i.e.  an improvement of the status quo, accomplishing the mandate, and an end to the conflict are among the possible benchmarks.  But g enerally, peacekeeping missions  are considered to have had diverse  results in Africa, with a few considered more su ccessful . Mathieu Duchâtel, Richard Gowan  and Manuel Lafont Rapnouil‘s research exposes the complexity and difficulties faced by Peacekeepers in Mali. They explains China’s presence in Africa as fostering peace in the territory and reducing terrorism as China contributes more to peace operations and building its overseas military base.  But the rebels doesn’t respect peacekeepers presence and even attack them as they considers their actions as corrupt  and  working hand in hand with the government they judge as  corrupt. An  example as stated by the authors is an incident of the  31st May  2016, “…Islamist insurgents attacked a United Nations base in Mali, killing one peacekeeper and wounding 12 others. Such hit-and-run raids are common, and have killed 65 UN personnel since 2013. But while most of the victims have been poorly equipped African troops, this time the dead soldier and five of the injured were Chinese”. Han Dorussen and  Theodora-Ismene Gizelis  argues on the effectiveness of multidimensional peacekeeping and conflict resolution as it is much more complex than traditional peacekeeping  (D. a.-I. Gizelis 2013) .  The argument goes further to acknowledge the fact that good governance  should reduce the risk of conflict  but reducing risk if conflict does not necessarily means there will probably be an  end to direct violent conflict.  This is because generally after a conflict situations what usually remains at the beginning is a high level of dis trust  toward authorities as for the case of Mali’s  central authority, generally pe rceived as corrupt .  Hence  trus t in central institutions can be perceived as one of the  key ingredients for successful  peace building .    According to Helen Scanlon and Tim Murithi, “The Congo crisis of 1960 to 1964 also led to the deployment of the largest and most complex UN mission at the time with 20,000 troops”.    They relate the fact that there is a great obstruction to Peacekeeping missions in Congo due to lack of political will, of resources and absence of clarity on questions of mandate. In his  paper «The  United Nations’ Role in Africa” (UN Peacekeeping in the Horn of Africa: Problems and Prospect), he demonstrated that the UN needs to adapt a longer-term strategy to build its peacekeeping operations. So according to Tadesse, “the UN had to demilitarize, or “civilianize”, national and inter-clan politics to ensure effective demobilization”.  Fundamentally looking at these peacekeeping missions on these regions, we suggest that the UN needed or need to adopt a common frame to provide a model for conflict-related interventions in Africa. To ensure effective evolution, he proposed the UN should also foster co-operation among state actors and regional security arrangements. The fact that terrorist doesn’t respect humanitarian int e rventions and attack those forces hinders  peace and even the fruitfulness of interventions by other nations  and if they have no pity for these  interventions , no one wants to think  about  civilians .  Looking at the China cooperation in Mali ;  the   incre ased risks associated with such deployments can be  perceived in  the fatalities China has suf fered in Mali for example; the incident of the   1st June  2016 when a  Chinese peacekeeper died  and four others were injured wh en their base in Gao was  attacked .   Despite  such incidents,  China still provides considerable support to the Malian government as well as the operational capacity of Mali's  defense  and security forces and majority of Malians see China as having greatest external influence in their country, after France . The resear ch of Isiaka Alani Badmus  looked at the complexity of establishing  a UN Peacekeeping operation in Burundi. It explains  the  fact that the  UN was at first reluctant to establish a peacekeeping operation in Burundi  base on collaboration with regional  organizations   while there  was still a th reat for conflict to degenerate and for this reason, the AU was urged to  establish  a mission in Burundi  known as  (AMIB) in 2003 under South African leadership and also involving troops from Ethiopia and Mozambique.   The role of this mission was very  important in  setting   conditions through which peace  cou ld be built in the country.  AMIB  proof its efficacy  in de-e scalating a potentially crucial situation which  enhanced a  UN evaluation team  on the mission on February 2004; through which it was  concluded that the conditions were appropriate to establish a UN peacekeeping operation in the country.   So, f ollowing a UN Security Council decision to deploy a peacekeeping mission to Burundi in May   2004, the  UN Secretary-General  at that time,  Kofi Annan appointed a Special Representative, Berhanu Dinka, to head the mission in June 2004. The former AMIB troops were incorporated into the UN Peace Operation in Burundi (ONUB). By November 2006, some 20,000 Burundian military personnel had been  demobilized , but many lacked economic opportunities and thus still pos e a potential security threat”  (Basagic 2007) . This evidently led to conflict de-escalation  in spite  efforts made to avoid conflict to persist as it was  too early to conclude whether t he foundations  laid by both  AMIB and ONUB will be judged  sustainable .  The UN has seen relative peacekeeping success in Namibia and Mozambique, and, eventually, Sierra Leone. Looking at the case of  Sierra Leone  in the  (Cosmopolitan Peacekeeping and Peacebuilding in Sierra Leone: What Can Africa Contribute? ) ,  David Curran and Tom Woodhouse  discusses on the fact that  UN peacekeeping operations were defined by differences in approaches on how to address the conflict .  “ In terms of peacekeeping missions, the United Nations  Mission in Sierra Leone grew from 6,000 (1999) to 20,000  (Woodhouse, Cosmopolitan Peacekeeping and Peacebuilding in Sierra Leone: What Can Africa Contribute ?  2007) . In addition, there were internal divisions within UNAMSIL on how to manage the conflict. French interests in managing the dispute also affected the UN peacekeeping mission in Côte d’Ivoire ” .   Overall, t he UN’s experience of peacekeeping in West Africa demonstrates the tensions as well as the potential involved in establishing a powerful partnership between UN actors and regional  organizations  in Africa. The source base of the study Based on “Brahimi’s report on the Changing Nature of UN Peace keeping Operations of August 2000” (named after Algerian UN Special Envoy, Lakhdar Brahimi), An analysis of previous  UN  peacekeeping  missions disclosed   a sharp increase in both the complexity and frequency of missions since the end of the Cold War.  " The United Nations undertook just 15 peace operations around the world between 1948 and 1988 and out of those 15, only three missions received mandates that led to ceasefire and force separation ” .  Since the era of decolonization from the 1950s, Africa has been ravaged by a series of conflicts  and this report  states that the most prominent UN presence in Africa has been through its peacekeeping missions since 1956 with the Suez Canal crisis, which saw the creation of the world’s first-ever peacekeeping mission. The Brahimi’s report recognized the need to improve collaboration between the UN and Africa’s regional orga nizations. Also, greater efforts was  made to support a stronger relationship between the UN and regional organizations and to consider linking regional peacekeeping capacities to the UN peacekeeping system. Given that the data is a count of yearly  changes over time for different activities, quantitative methods were chosen as the most appropriate to identify trends in the data and compare the evolution through tim e. We will try  to understand  if current findings on the evolution Peacekeeping operations in Mali can or should be revisited or not. Taking into  account the MINUSMA in Mali. The theoretical and methodological approaches to research peacekeeping The conception of Peacekeeping and main direction of its evolution. According C. Kuhl, Chief of Peacekeeping Best Practices Section, United Nations , ‘peace operations’ can be referred as  a wide variety  of peace and security interventions in in ternational conflict management which involves different actors  such as the United Nations, the African Union, the European Union, NATO and other regional and sub-regional organizations, as well as multi-national forces  and , which  are not under an organizational development but can be guided to work for the same purpose .   Trevor Findlay  ascertains the fact that ‘‘the term peacekeeping has been invented long after praxis had  begun  and improvisation” Peacekeeping generally refers to the deployment of international personnel to help maintain peace and security.  According to  Eric G. Boris , t he Blue  Helmet defines  peacekeeping as “an operation involving military personnel, but without enforcement powers. Undertaken by the United Nations to help maintain or restore international peace and security in areas of conflict ”.  Boutros-Ghali   further defines peace keeping as “the deployment of a UN presence in the field, hitherto with the consent of all parties concerned, normally involving military or police personnel and frequently civilians as well” .   Some studies of peacekeeping include efforts to contain or  terminate hostilities  in a given conflictual area , whereas others   collides  the definition  “… to efforts to prevent the recurrence of war once a  ceasefir e is in place” as explained by the authors .  There are many concepts in the peacekeeping field that have more-or-less the same meaning, but they often reflect important differences in their re spective doctrinal approaches.  “Peacekeeping”  traditionally referred  in UN Peacekeeping Operations of Capstone’s doctrine are  operations where lightly armed soldiers were deployed as a neutral 3rd party cease-fire monitoring group between two clearly identifiable opposing forces ’ . While in the 1990s the Brahimi report introduced a new term ‘Peace Operations’ which were meant to reflect the new multidimensional post-Cold War UN operations that were tasked with supporting the implementation of comprehensive peace agreements .   The  C apstone doctrine of UN peacekeeping of 2008 reflects decades of UN experience with peacekeeping operations and will be a great asset to provide a  positive   trajectory to  new  UN peacekeeping personnel.  It provides  a contemporary understanding of how practitioners might apply the UN’s three basic peacekeeping principles, namely: consent, impartiality and non-use of force, except in  self-defense  and  defense  of the mandate.  ‘Consent’ by the parties to the peace- or cease-fire agreement, is a  dynamic concept  which is essential for mission success and must be constantly managed, but it is understood that it may often be lacking at the tactical level.  ‘Impartiality’ means that the mandate must be applied without  favor  or prejudice to the parties to the peace agreement, and should not be confused with ‘neutrality’. It must be just.  ‘Non-use of force’ is refers to the strategic level, i.e. Non-use of peace enforcement.  According to Capstone, the UN is good at undertaking consent based operations, but introduce the concept ‘robust peacekeeping’ to signify recognition that the use of force at the tactical level may be necessary to defend the mission and its mandate from spoilers, and to protect civilians (when mandated) “ However, as peacekeeping operations are ultimately a tool in the hands of the UN Security Council, and as the mandates given to specific missions are generated through political processes  that require compromise and trade-offs, real-world peacekeeping operations are not likely to always fit the exact definitions or measure up to the success factors formulated in the  doctrine .  Due to its unique international character, and the powers vested in its founding Charter, the Organization can take action on a wide range of issues, and provide a forum for its 193 Member States to express their views, through the General Assembly, the Security Council, the Economic and Social Council and other bodies and committees. According A. Abashidze and D.Vidineyev , peacekeeping  operations have traditionally invoked the UN Charter where the Security Council  carries primary  responsibility for maintaining peace and security but has to take into account a number of factors when deploying its missions. The authors when answering the question of who send Peacekeepers? Makes us understand that the United Nations does not have its own personal armed forces or police but when requesting  for forces  to intervene during conflict, the military and police joins for the peacekeeping operation at the request of the United Nations.  “ Peacekeepers wear the military uniform of their respective countries, and their affiliation with the UN peacekeepers is reflected only by their blue helmets or berets and identification insignia ” .  The work of the United Nations reaches every corner of the globe. Although best known for peacekeeping,  peace building , conflict prevention and humanitarian assistance, there are many other ways the United Nations and its System (specialized agencies, funds and programs) affect our lives and make the world a better place. The Organization works on a broad range of fundamental issues, from sustainable development, environment and refugees protection, disaster relief, counter terrorism, disarmament and non-proliferation, to promoting democracy, human rights, gender equality and the advancement of women, governance, economic and social development and international health, clearing landmines, expanding food production, and more, in order to achieve its goals and coordinate efforts for a safer world for this and future generations. The scope of peace and security activities  A legal framework of peacekeeping is based the Charter of the United Nation. The Charter of the United Nations was signed, in San Francisco, on 26 June 1945 and is the foundation document for all the United Nations work. The United Nations was established to “save succeeding generations from the scourge of war” and one of its main purposes is to maintain international peace and security.     Peacekeeping, although not explicitly provided for in the Charter, has evolved into one of the main tools used by the United Nations to achieve this purpose.    The Charter gives the United Nations Security Council primary responsibility for the maintenance of international peace and security. In fulfilling this responsibility, the Security Council may adopt a range of measures, including the establishment of a United Nations peacekeeping operation.     The legal basis for such action is found in Chapters VI, VII and VIII of the Charter. While Chapter VI deals with the “Pacific Settlement of Disputes”, Chapter VII contains provisions related to “Action with Respect to the Peace, Breaches of the Peace and Acts of Aggression”.  Chapter VIII of the Charter also provides for the involvement of regional arrangements and agencies in the maintenance of international peace and security.  “ United Nations peacekeeping operations have traditionally been associated with Chapter VI of the Charter. However, the Security Council need not refer to a specific Chapter of the Charter when passing a resolution authorizing the deployment of a United Nations peacekeeping operation and has never invoked Chapter VI. In recent years, the Security Council has adopted the practice of invoking Chapter VII of the Charter when authorizing the deployment of United Nations peacekeeping operations into volatile post- conflict settings where the State is unable to maintain security and public order. The Security Council’s invocation of Chapter VII in these situations, in addition to denoting the legal basis for its action, can also be seen as a statement of firm political  resolve and a means of reminding the parties to a conflict and the wider United Nations membership of their obligation to give effect to Security Council decisions ” . The United Nations is an international organization founded in 1945 after the Second World War by 51 countries committed to maintaining international peace and security, developing friendly relations among nations and promoting social progress, better living standards and human rights.  Peacekeeping is considered as one of the main tools used by the United Nations to achieve its  purpose to  “save succeeding generations from the scourge of war ” to  maintain international peace and security  (Doctrine 2008) .   It has unique strengths, including legitimacy, burden sharing, and an ability to deploy and sustain troops and police from around the globe, integrating them  sometimes  with civilian peacekeepers to advance multidimensional mandates. The UN Peacekeeping is guided by three basic principles: Consent of the parties, Impartiality and Non-use of force except in  self-defense  and  defense  of the mandate.               Peacekeeping is distinguished from  peace building , peacemaking,   peace enforcement  and  conflict prevention although the United Nations acknowledges the fact that all activities mutually works together and overlaps between them is frequent in practice.   We should be careful while s eparating these concepts  because we have to keep in mind the fact that  the re is not a firm boundary  between these  concepts . The  UN defines the peacekeeping as “a technique designed to preserve the peace, however fragile, where fighting has been halted, and to assist in implementing agreements achieved by the peacemakers. Over the years, peacekeeping has evolved from a primarily military model of observing cease-fires and the separation of forces after inter-state wars, to incorporate a c omplex model of many elements military, police and civilian   working together to help lay the foundations for sustainable peace” .   So  multidimensional  peaceke eping operations are used  not only to maintain peace and security, but also to facilitate the political process, protect civilians, assist in  the disarmament  and reintegration of former combatants; support the organization of elections, protect and promote human rights and assist in restoring the rule of law. As far as  Conflict prevention  is concern,  generally it is seen as  a diplomatic approach that refers to a variety of activities and strategies within  peace building  fields that are deployed to pre- empt and subsequently  neutralize  potential triggers to widespread violent conflict.  I t  involves the application of structural or diplomatic measures to keep intra-state or inter-state tensions and disputes from escalating into violent conflict    , thereby making societies ’  resilient to violent conflict by strengthening the local capacities for peace  through  systems, resources, structures, attitudes, skills .   According to Chapter VI of the UN  Charter,  peacemaking as a non-restrictive list of peaceful, diplomatic, and judicial means of resolving disputes. It  generally includes measures to address conflicts in progress and usually involves diplomatic action to bring hostile parties to a negotiated agreement.  In his conceptual platform known as the “Agenda for Peace” , Boutros Boutros-Ghali list ed some elements through which  peacemaking is represented  i.e.  through activities such as mediation, conciliation, and judicial settlement. Peace enforcement involves the application, with the authorization of the Security Council, of a range of coercive measures, including the use of military force.  It activities are generally used to create a peace from a broken ceasefire or to enforce a peace demanded by the United Nations. And it requires more military force as compared to Peace keeping and is thereby best done by heavily armed forces .     Peace building   is a long term collaborative process aim to prevent the outbreak, escalation, continuation and recurrence of conflict, so can take place before, during and after conflicts. It employ s a range of measures targe ting  to reduce the risk of  diving  into conflict by strengthening national capacities at all levels for conflict management, and to lay the foundation for sustainable peace and development. It address   the root  of  violent conf lict in a comprehensive manner . Besides UN peacekeeping, there are two other types of third-party peacekeeping missions: peacekeeping missions by regional inter-go vernmental organizations (since  1945) and peac ekeeping missions by states .  These  inter-governmental organizations vary from multi-regional organizations such as the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) and the Commonwealth of Nations (CON) to sub-regional organizations such as the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) and the Commonwealth of Independent States  (CIS). This category also includes organizations with members located primarily in one particular region of the world, such as the Organization of American States (OAS) in the Western Hemisphere and the Organization of African Unity (OAU) in Africa The several peacekeeping missions have been established by s tates  outside of the official auspices of the UN or a regional inter-governmental organizations. This category includes peacekeeping missions consisting of personnel from one state (unilateral peacekeeping) and peacekeeping missions consisting of personnel from two or more states (multilateral peacekeeping) . While previous studies of UN peacekeeping have contributed much descriptive information about such missions, they have contributed little theoretical understanding of the conditions under which party actors are motivated to establish peacekeeping missions. It is interesting to examine UN peacekeeping from various theoretical perspectives to yield insights about how international relations are changing and developing. Whereas Liberals mainly concentrate on how lasting peace and cooperation in international relations can be ensured. They believe material power is not the sole determinant of international relations and  emphasize  the role of international institutions in promoting and enforcing peaceful relations among nations by mitigating the implications of existing anarchy .  “… International organizations act as higher authority since the states accept willingly to limit their own power, autonomy, and, to some extent, freedom through a set of rules put in place by international organizations of which they become members ” .     Also, they tend to perceive conflict as a prob lem that needs to be fixed . New idea of  “Adaptive  peace building  recognizes that conflict is a normal and necessary element of change. Liberal theory raises the  following   questions ;   A re international institutions completely independent from the influence of their stron gest members or are they mostly  mechanisms manipulated by powerful states?  Why are some  peacekeeping mandates  wider and  disorganized than  others ? Does the change  in mandate flexibi lity reflect the needs to resolve  conflict or the politica l interests of powerful states ? At the tactical level, issues are identified in terms of a common understanding of the operation, cooperation, discipline, and code of conduct. Discipline or code of conduct. This proved to be more  challenging b ecause of  the  Malian  state officials  realized their main  obstacle to national unity was directed toward  the northern popul ation of Mali . The southern elites  egoistically  resolved to focus their political and  economic efforts on the  south of Mali, gradually  marginalizing  the  north of the country . As the north seeked representation, h istorically rooted distrust had led to four Tuareg and Arab uprisings following independence: in 1963, 1991, 2006 and  the straw that broke the camel’s back  was in   2012. Even though peace agreements were signed and multiple  demobilization   programs  implemented, none of them succeeded in  consolidating  the relationship b etween the north and the capital . In order to prevent one group from gaining too much influence and posing a threat to central state authority, Bamako sought to  take advantage by dividing the northern   and exploit interethnic and sectarian tensions to impose its authority. Among the instruments of this strategy were the use of electoral zoning  to  favor  certain Tuareg tribes over other groups or the Arab community.  What resulted from  this strategy   were  animosity  betwe en northern populations,  resentment by communities towards the central state, and a very heterogeneous distribution of needs and demands that made inclusive peace talks and agreements far harder to reach. Indeed, Tuareg, Arab,  Songhai  or F ulani northern people do not share the same  traditions. They do not all  recognize  ‘Azawad’ as their common land, or the necessity of independence. Ara bs and Tuaregs for instance;  have long been in confrontation with the central state;  . The divisions between northern populations is a crucial element in understanding the difficulty in converting post-conflict stability into anything like su stainable peace. Furthermore, intra-Malian tensions and community distrust have been aggravated by foreign interference and other, more passive, forces who have served to aid and abet tensions in the north. By taking advantage of the general weakness of the Malian state, these external actors have sought to build their in fluence primarily in the north. The north of Mali raised  attention  of  countries like Libya and Algeria. The International Response This section directs attention toward international efforts to g rapple with the issues that the  previous section addressed. The section offers an overview of, firs t, the history of international  development efforts, second, international engagement in the wake of  the 2012 uprising, and  third,  ongoing activities  in the country  to help from the scourge of war. Although   Mali  is  insecure and  its neighbors (Niger, Burkina Fas o, and Cote d’Ivoire) have  been attacked by violent extremist groups based there, the internation al military interventions helpe d Mali and the West African region to a large extent from the disastrous consequences of Isla mist control of the country. To  better understand these interventions,  and appreciate their  achievements, and shortcomings,  a brief note on  the  dynamics  of the northern Mali conflict will be helpful. The nor th of Mali has been seized  by th e Tuaregs, an ethnic group  found in the Saharan parts of Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso, Algeria, and Libya. Living in Mali since independence in 1960, the Tuareg people have suffered from political and economic marginalization. Despite bloody repressions by governmental armed forces, several international interventions, and negotiated peace agreements, they have waged repeated armed insurgencies against the central government in Bamako, most notably in 1962,  1990, 2006, and  2012.  Since 2012, the Tuareg secessionists have been joined by extremist groups claiming to wage jihad for the creation of an Islamic state in northern Mali, where arms, drugs, hostage taking, and human trafficking have become a source of income for criminal networks, while causi ng insecurity in the territory . At t he root of the recurrent Tuareg  led rebellions and the jihadist movement are ethnic and religious as well as economic, political, and social concerns. Though the jihadist groups share the religious co ncerns of the  Tuareg,  th eir  agendas  differs and this  make s  the conflict more complex and difficult to resolve. Instead of focusing on the Tua reg rebellion, this section concentrates on the  international mi litary interventions against  the violent extremist organizations in northern Mali, which are considered terrorist groups, namely Ansar Dine, Al-Qaeda in Islamic Maghreb (AQIM), Al Mourabitoun, the Macina Liberation Front, and the Movement for Oneness and Jihad in Wes t Africa . The main forms of international intervention in intrastate conflicts are sanctions, mediation, and the use of force under UN cha pter VII .  These have all taken place in northern Mali under the leadership of multilateral organizations a nd major powers, notably France . The various regional and international interventions in the Mali crisis  since 2012 have been confluent  strategies that emerge d from an international decision s to support the local authorities in their efforts to preserve Mali’s territorial integrity, to protect human rights, uphold political stability and the democratic principles of governance, and  fight terrorism . As compared  to conflicts like the one in Syria, where  some permanent members of the UN  Security Council  opposed  to the  idea,  in the case of Africa,  traditional and emerging powers as well as among African regional organizations  consented  on the necessity to intervene in th e Mali conflict. This was mainly driven by two  elements: first ly b y the focus of these interventions on fighting jihadist terrorism and defending Mali’s sovereignty and territorial integ rity and secondly b y the explicit request of Mali’s authori ties .  The African and French-led Military Interventions In response to UN Security Council Resolution adopted in December 2012, and the request of the Malian government, France launched Operation Serval on January 11, 2013. This operation, which lasted until July 2014, resulted in the deployment of 4,500 French soldiers and African troops.   It  had three main objectives:    Assisting  Mali’s army to stop the progress of the armed groups toward Bamako Helping Mali preserve the integrity and unity of its territory by regaining control of the norther n cities , and  Facilitating the implementation of international decisions through the deployment of two complementary missions, the African-led International Support Mission to Mali (AFISMA) which was charged with helping to strengthen Mali’s defense and security  forces in coordination with the EU and other partners; supporting the Malian authorities in their primary responsibility to protect the population, transitioning activities following the recovery national authority and assisting with humanitarian access to the northern part of Mali.  And  the European Union Training Mission (EUTM) for Mali’s armed forces which goal was to provide military training to members of the Malian armed forces; strengthen the capabilities of the Malian armed forces capable of contributing to the defense of their territory. There was a high risk that letting the Tuareg and jihadist groups take control of  the whole of Mali would have conceded  the Sahara -Sahel region into the hands of  Al-Qaeda-affiliated groups and a source of major t hreat to international security.  Like Afghanistan under the Taliban regime, Mali was under severe risk of falling under the control of jihadists. Because of the gross human rights violations, the threat to Mali’s territorial integrity, and the risk of regional and international  insecurity, the UN Secur ity Council, ECOWAS, and the AU  supported Operation Serval .  Operation Serval reversed the dynamics of the conflict in favor of Mali’s army, which up to that point had been failing to contain  the armed insurgency . The French-led military intervention led to the liberation of the main towns under the control  of Islamist groups . The jihadist groups were substantially weakened as they suffered heavy losses. The Sharia rules imposed by the jihadists were reversed and overall security improved for the general population. Elections were also held, leading to the restoration of the rule of law and the strengthening  of democracy in Mali as a whole .  In terms of the objectives set by France, Operation Serval may seem successful.  However, a more critical assessment of the international intervention in Mali may offer a different picture.  Though the jihadists seemed to be defeated within Mali in 2013, there were five failings of the French intervention logic that suggest that this was a tactical rather than a strategic defeat.  The French intervention was based on the vision that the problem in Mali is about Jihadist terrorism rather than a chronic domestic political crisis. Secondly, the continued deterioration of the security situation reported by UN sources in the north proved that the French, after recapturing the territory, could not protect the re-conquered territory against the unconventional tactics of a dispersed terrorist adversary.  The third shortcoming of the French military intervention was its attempt to destroy highly mobile transnational armed groups with a campaign limited to one country that has completely open borders, which simply displaced the jihadist problem into neighboring  countries.   French-led military interventions, especially Operation Barkhane, relied on t he partnership of an  authoritarian regimes   such as the regime of President Idriss Deby in Chad, for the implementation of its counter-terrorism strategy . Overall, instead of strengthening democratic governance, it rather contributed to the strengthening of undemocratic regimes in the Sahel. Based on the above  shortcomings, a ddressing these consequences of a flawed military intervention strategy in order to build more sustainable peace and security in Mali and the Sahel will not be easy or quick. A political solution to the domestic conflict in northern Mali is the key element that was stymied by the French military intervention, and for which the responsibility lies very much with the elected  Malian governm ent. Tramond and Seigneur    assessment shows that the French-led military intervention in Mali was not as successful as claimed . In fact, they  acknowledged that  “stability in Mali will need a political settlement, not only between southern and northern ethnic groups but also within the Malian Army itself” and that “a mission is not over until the last unit is dismissed into its barracks and every piece of equipment has been  turned in and accounted for ”.   The limited and temporary nature of the French success in Mali explains why Operation Serval was combined with the Chad-based Operation Epervier into a multilateral anti-terrorist operation with three th ousand troops .   Operation  Barkhane was launched on August 1, 2014 with the dual mission of supporting the G5 Sahel countries in their fight against terrorism and also preventing the reconstitution of terrorist sanctuar ies in the Sahara-Sahel  region.   This was an important mission aimed at complementing the UN peacekeeping mission in Mali. The UN Peacekeeping and Stabilization Mission To pursue the work started by Operation Serval, a United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali (MINUSMA) was established by Security Council Resolution 2100 in April 2013. The mandate of MINUSMA, renewed each year since 2013, has included ensuring security and stability in the country, protecting civilians, monitoring and promoting human rights, and supporting national dialogue and reconciliation. However, counter-terrorism is not pa rt of the mission . As t he mission continued to pursue its objectives , there have been growing calls to  increase troops and equipment .  Given the security and stabilization needs of Mali, one may wonder whether the size of  the  peacekeeping force deployed by MINUSMA, the mandate it was given, and, more generally, the traditional peacekeeping mandate could have met the needs of the local population in such a protracted political and ethno-religious conflict. Peace, for example, was not really restored in Sierra Leone until the international community took a clear stance against Revolutionary United Front (RUF) . This intervention  protected civilians from the rebels and mobilized resources to promot e postwar reconstruction , security  and justice .  MINUSMA has greatly contributed to the protection of civilians in northern Mali and the stabilization of the country, but the intervention had shortcomings that are inherently related to the limitations of the current international regime for dealing with violent conflicts. One example that illustrates this state of affairs is the difficulty that the UN always has to mobilize human and  financial resources in a timely and sufficient manner for peacekeeping missions.   In spite of the deployment of this sizable peacekeeping force, complaints about insecurity in the north and other parts of Mali have persisted. In a briefing to the UN Security Council, the commander of the UN peacekeeping force in Mali indicated that his force,  “ which has been repeatedly targeted by jihadists, did not have the mandate, the training, and the right equipment to engage in anti-terrorist o r counter-insurgency operations” .  At least 168  UN peacekeepers have been killed and 166 injured in Mali since 2003, which makes MINUSMA one of the most perilous UN peacekeeping missions . In a hostile environment where armed insurgents violate human rights and international humanitarian laws by targeting everyone, including civilians and UN peacekeepers, there has been no peace to  keep despite the consent on  cease-fire. MINUSMA mandate   structure and  Protection of Civilians (POC) Civilian protection and conflict resolution are critical components of peacekeeping mission activities in Mali. This section explores the evolution of peacekeeping efforts in Mali, specifically focusing on the approaches and initiatives aimed at civilian protection and conflict resolution. By examining the progress made, challenges faced, and lessons learned, we can gain insights into the evolving role of peacekeeping missions in safeguarding civilians and promoting conflict resolution in Mali. This  part provides  a brief overview of the importance of civilian protection  in conflict resolution in Mali so  we can better understand how civilian protection contributes to fostering peace, stability, and sustainable solutions in Mali.   The Importance of Civilian Protection in Conflict Resolution: A Brief Overview in Mali  Safeguarding Human  Rights :   Civilian protection is essential in conflict resolution as it upholds the fundamental human rights of individuals affected by armed conflict. B y ensuring the safety,  and well-being of civilians, their basic rights, including the right to life, security, and freedom from violence, are protected. Such protection creates an environment conducive to dialogue, reconciliation, and sustainable peace.  Mitigating Humanitarian  Impact:   Conflict inflicts tremendous humanitarian suffering on civilians, including displacement, loss of livelihoods, and limited access to essential services. Prioritizing civilian protection helps mitigate the impact of conflict on vulnerable populations, ensuring their immediate needs, such as shelter, food, water, and healthcare, are addressed. This not only saves lives but also enhances the prospects for peaceful coexistence and long-term stability.  Building Trust and Reconciliation:   Effective civilian protection fosters trust and confidence among conflict-affected communities. When civilians feel safe and protected, they are more likely to engage in dialogue and reconciliation processes. By creating an environment conducive to trust-building, civilian protection initiatives contribute to fostering social cohesion, addressing grievances, and promoting reconciliation between different groups, thereby paving the way for sustainable peace.    Addressing Root Causes of  Conflict :   Civilian protection is not just about addressing the immediate impact of conflict but also about addressing its root causes. By protecting civilians, conflict resolution efforts can focus on addressing the underlying socio-political, economic, and structural factors that contribute to conflict. Addressing these root causes is crucial for sustainable peace, as it helps prevent the recurrence of violence in the long term.  Empowering Local  Communities :   Effective civilian protection in conflict resolution recognizes the agency and resilience of local communities. By involving and empowering local communities in protection strategies, decision-making processes, and  peace building  initiatives, their perspectives and needs are  acknowledged. This empowerment contributes to more inclusive and sustainable conflict resolution processes, rooted in the aspirations and priorities of the affected communities.  International Norms and Obligations:  Civilian protection in conflict resolution is not only essential from a moral standpoint but also mandated by international legal norms and obligations. International humanitarian law and human rights frameworks oblige state and non-state actors to protect civilians during armed conflicts. The concept recognizes the importance of ensuring that civilians are not subjected to violence, abuses, or violation s of their human rights. A New Environment for UN Peace Operations The implicati ons of the  UN efforts to r eestablish sustainable security and  promote effective governan ce are deep . The deployment of a UN peacekeeping mission to Mali has raised concerns about whether the United Nations is prepared to operate in areas threatened by transnational terrorist groups linked to AL  Qaeda.  Extremist Islamist groups challenge the existing  world order and the UN Charter . The 1648 Peace of Westphalia, which ended the Thirty Years War, created an international order based on individual and sovereign nation-states. The United Nations is an international organization composed of member states. In contrast , al -Qaida seek to establish a global caliphate that would replace the existing international state structure. Extremist Islamist groups ignore national borders and can disperse and reassemble in vast areas like the Sahel that are beyond the c ontrol of national government. The fact that  extremist Islamist groups are driven by a  view of world order which  reject compromise based  on political concessions, they  objectively rejects the  traditional UN approaches to negotiated solutions. Transnational Islamist terrorist organizations directly engage in criminal activities, such as kidnapping international workers for ransom acts as a conflict multiplier by financing spoilers, promoting corruption, and delegitimizing local government officials and security forces. UN Police, whose primary mission is restoring public order, cannot control cross-border trafficking or counter sophisticated criminal organizations bas ed outside their mission area.  Meanwhile, Islamist extremist groups have attacked UN facil ities and killed UN personnel.  An example; On October 3, 2014, nine UN peacekeepers were killed when militant gunmen ambushed their convoy. This incident brought the number of UN troops killed in Mali to thirty, with over ninety wounded since the start of the UN mission. Attacks on UN troops and police have negatively affected the willingness of troop contributing countries (TCCs) to contribute forces to UN missions.  This has required the United Nations to rely on the African Union (AU) and  sub-regional  organizations such as the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) that see threats to their national interests and are willing to engage in combat operations and suffer casualties. Terrorist attacks on the United Nations have affected the organization’s ability to recruit staff and interact with local counterparts. This study proposes John Paul Lederach’s theory on Conflict Transformation as an alternative strategy to rethink peacekeeping operations. Highlighting the importance of relationships to achieve reconciliation, it emphasizes the central role individuals must play to achieve peace.  Through the example of the conflict in Mali, this work concludes that there is the need to rethink peace operations as a whole because conflict still persist. This notion came from his Little Book of Conflict Transformation which came out in 2003. Though containing several great ideas, the part of the book I want to highlight what John Paul refer to as the big picture of conflict transformation, which is the diagram that's shown here.  (Appendix,  Figure  4 )   He divides any given conflict into three and refers to it as inquiries. The first one is the Presenting Situation, the second one the Horizon of the Future, and the third one Change Processes. His big picture of conflict transformation is generally use to map conflicts. In order to understand what's going on in any conflict we have to look first at the presenting situation and go beyond the surface level things, the issues i.e. dive in the deep er roots and levels of history.  How has the conflict we talk about  developed over time?  What dynamics have been taking place over time? What are the patterns of interaction and the patterns of relationship that have been goi ng on over time? To look in  deep er  into not just issues but, also history and patterns of interaction.  Most often people think  about resolving issues   in terms of  bringing  solutions, and think that they can jump directly from issues to solutions. But you'll notice, in this diagram  there is no direct pattern  to direct solution . The reason is that the solutions are more complicate d  to reach and sometimes  there is the need to develope  change processes. So the big picture is a circle that  regards  the horizon of the future, it  has three elements. There's the obvious solution, but then there's als o relationships and  systems where  there is the  need to understand how people are going to relate to each other, how the dynamics of the system are going to be different from what was going on in the presenting situation that will enable this new situation, the new system to be more constructive than the current one. In the bottom on the development of change processes, he focuses on four different kinds of change: personal change of individuals, relational change, changes in the way people relate to each other and groups relate to each other, structural change, changes in government structure and education structure and health structures and all sorts of structural things that might be impeding more constructive ways of interacting, and cultural changes ways in which current cultural norms are inhibiting conflict resolution and make changes to those norms or changes to behaviors that will be more constructive in the future. Th e light colored arrow in the center shows that this is all a continuing cycle so that if you make a change tha t leads to new systems, it will have an impact .  The explanation of the diagram is very  dynamic as it  shows change  in  the way people and relationships and history relate to each other, how they change over time, and how any change has influence forwards and to some e xtent backwards. So we  find  this analysis very useful as it can contribute in  underst and ing the Malian conflict. The case of Mali reveals critical challenges that are pervasive in many international security promotion efforts in Africa. Too often, international interventions aimed at combating terrorism fail to fully take into account domestic economic, political, and identity drivers of the violence. Moreover, international interventions tend to have missions that are not well aligned with the local roots of the political violence. Mali is a very good c ase of where terrorism is related  to  historical  political and ec onomic marginalization problems . The case of Mali points to the importance of addressing socioeconomic grievances, aligning peacekeeping missions with the root causes of the conflict and promoting local ownership of the solutions to the conflict.  Sec urity and peace are still blurred  all over the country because of the p ersistence of terrorist attacks and non-consensus  with the  cease-fire agreement .   Although the use of force and multilateral peacekeeping are necessary to protect civilians, they are unlikely to end jihadist terrorism. This is because national and multilateral forces a re trained to fight  in a n environment they think they master with  traditional warfare mindset within an identifiable territory whereas the enemy is a non-state acto r, who is difficult to identify . Though some militants joined jihadist groups in northern and central Mali for security reasons to seek protection for themselve s, their families, and property,  lack of economic opportunities, local conflicts and grievances, and the weakness and absence of the state in these parts of the country  was also what fueled them the most . The challenge here is first to explore ways of promoting a change of attitude and behavior by identifying and stopping jihadist preachers, leaders, or movements that inspire terrorist attacks.  Second, even though not all militants join violent extremist groups be cause they became radicals , another challenge is to identify strategies that prove to be the most effective for tracking and preventing radicalization through the rigorous evaluation of programs designed to address its factors, such as youth unemployment, poor religious education, religious intolerance, and frustrations due to feelings of exclusion and marginalization in some regions or among certain ethnic groups.  Key Lesso ns  from the UN intervention in Mali to enlighten peacekeeping missions           Three critical lessons may be drawn from the UN experience in Mali to enlighten future peacekeeping missions, especially in the areas of communication with the local people, mandate design, and strategy for fighting terrorist groups. - First, it should be observed that ignorance of the decision-making processes at the UN and MINUSMA’s mandate has generated unrealistic expectations and negative reactions among the local population toward the UN peacekeeping operation. But, on this topic, Mali is not unique because negative reactions toward UN peacekeeping missions were also observed in other war-torn African countries such as the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Côte d’Ivoire where local people often expected too much from the UN peace operation and became disappointed. These misunderstandings between UN peacekeeping missions and local populations highlight the great challenge of communicating more effectively with the local people about what the UN and its peace operations can do. For anyone who knows the UN system, how it works, and its inherent limitations limited funding, little leverage on local actors, disagreements among the Security Council members in making decisions, and lack of a permanent standing force, to name but a few—is more likely to understand why it was difficult to deploy peacekeeping troops in the early moments of the crisis in 2012 before it escalated and why a UN peacekeeping operation in the current state of affairs is unable to meet certain expectations.  - Second, given the new threats and challenges facing peacekeeping missions in conflict zones such as northern Mali, the international community is challenged to revisit the way mandates are designed as well as the UN’s strategy for fighting jihadist terrorism. As the force commanders of the UN missions in South Sudan and Mali and the Chief of Staff of the UN Truce Supervision Organization (UNTSO) told the Security Council in June 2015, the experience of the  UN peacekeeping in Mali and in other war-torn countries clearly suggests that specially trained and better equipped peacekeepers are needed to deal with terrorist and other asymmetric threats. This recommendation must be, however, critically examined in light of one of the conclusions reached by a high-level independent panel of experts that UN peacekeeping missions, due to their composition and character, are not suited to engage in military counter-terrorism operations. “They lack the specific equipment, intelligence, logistics, capabilities and specialized military preparation required, among other aspects. Such operations should be undertaken by the host government or by a capable regional force or an ad hoc coalition authorized by the Security Council”    - Third, besides the emergence of new threats, the increasing demand for UN peacekeeping, and the growing number of fatalities among peacekeepers suggests that the UN is perhaps challenged to rethink the traditional method of acquiring, training, and deploying its peacekeeping personnel. For example, peacekeeping personnel in Africa could be recruited by African states or regional organizations ECOWAS, G5 Sahel, or African Union and be given special training with the partnership of the international community. This special training, which might be a combination of police and military training for protection and offensive operations, would provide these troops with the capacity, skills, and equipment required to deal with armed insurgents and jihadists as well as to closely protect civilians. Recruits from UN member-states in war-torn regions like Africa could be offered this special training under UN leadership with logistical, technical, and financial contributions from the international community. This would provide a permanent force, readily available for deployment in conflict zones. There is already the African Standby Force, which can serve as a starting point. The critical challenge, however, has been establishing a transnational framework with effective funding and decision-making mechanisms and a new warfare approach to tackle the security issues posed by terrorist and criminal networks, which operate across national borders and fight unconventional warfare.  To sum up, the shortcomings of the UN peacekeeping and stabilization mission in Mali reveal the limitations of the UN itself in its attempt to provide effective security governance, particularly in countering threats to international peace that now mostly emerge within the borders of failing and impoverished sovereign states. It will not be enough to find more effective ways of recruiting, training, equipping, and deploying the peacekeeping personnel; the UN must also invent a more effective model of peacekeeping and broaden the traditional concept and mandate  of peacekeeping so as to uphold the demands of international humanitarian law and reduce the number of fatalities both among non-combatant civilians and peacekeepers in conflict zones Mali’s crisis continues to challenge the peacekeeping community.  We should note the following; 1.  Lack of AU Capacity Necessitated Handover to UN  Mission;   a  major challenge for the AU has been its dearth of monetary resources for peace support operations and consequent need to rely on outside donors. However, African countries were able to meet troop contribution requirements. The inability of these forces to respond contributed to Mali's decision to request assistance from France. 2.  Strategic Approach Complicated by Competing Priorities of Involved Parties ; Early in the Malian crisis, the AU articulated AFISMA’s Strategic Concept and engaged with all of the key elements of the crisis, including its transnational dimensions. This reflected the AU’s broad interests, which surpass the more constrained interests of the UN, and th e narrower interests of France.  Many of the political priorities were ultimately determined, not by a comprehensive strategy, but by bargaining over the resources available for peace operations, and the political priority of France to have what it considered a credible exit-and-handover strategy for its forces. 3.  Counter-Terrorism Mandate Poses Challenges for Successful  Peacekeeping;   while  the AU has been involved in multiple missions, MINUSMA is the first UN multidimensional peacekeeping mission to deploy in a context where transnational terrorism is present and countering that terrorism is part of its mission .  This is an extremely challenging environment, as appropriate responses to transnational threats differ from responses to domestic threats. As Mali’s governmental presence is not particularly robust in the north, groups are focusing their efforts on the most robust military security presence there, which is MINUSMA. 4.  Terrorist Groups Show Enhanced  Capacity;   though  violence is not escalating on a wide scale in Mali, attacks by terrorist groups continue to occur against civilians and have spread south to Bamako. In addition to AQIM, Ansar Dine, MUJAO, and al- Mourabitoun, a new group - the Macina Liberation Movem ent (FLM)  has emerged in Mali’s previously stable central and southern regions. The FLM has attacked military posts and been accused of civilian exe cutions throughout the  country.  While  the UN continues to emphasize that the implementation of the Peace and Reconciliation Agreement is proceeding, if violence continues to spread through central and  southern Mali, it is uncertain whether or not this agreement will be realized. Many agree that the crisis in Mali was a political problem; however, mediating a political solution, when one side is a transnational, jihadist group (AQIM), is difficult. Because government officials are targeted in attacks, reestablishing state  auto  Lessons Learned for Future Peacekeeping Operation.  The peacekeeping efforts in Mali have provided valuable lessons for fu ture peacekeeping missions. These  points above   can inform  and help to see beyond what is actually happening and the direction  the  conflict takes. CONCLUSION Preventing the UN Peacekeeping activities in Mali from falling into Irrelevance is a great challenge as the influence of peacekeeping activities in Mali is difficult to pinpoint due to the fact that conflict dynamics become more complicated and Mali’s mandate more ambitious. Knowing the impact of specific efforts is a challenge in all peacekeeping contexts , so as the research question  was;  to determine/reveal, reasons/factors of conflict progress/persistence regardless of international efforts to  deescalate the conflict in Mali, we should try to see  how the Lessons Learned can be  applied  to Future Peacekeeping Missions in Mali  and other regions .  1.1  Understanding the Local Context: Comprehensive understanding of the local context is crucial for effective peacekeeping. Future missions should invest in in-depth analysis of the political, social, and cultural dynamics specific to the region. This understanding helps identify root causes of the conflict, local actors, and potential risks and challenges.  1.2  Holistic Approach: Peacekeeping missions should adopt a holistic approach that encompasses security, development, and governance aspects. Addressing the underlying causes of conflict, such as poverty, inequality, and governance deficits, is essential for sustainable peace. Coordinated efforts between military, civilian, and humanitarian actors are necessary to achieve comprehensive  peace building .  1.3  Protection of Civilians: The protection of civilians should be a central priority in peacekeeping missions. Lessons from Mali highlight the importance of robust strategies and measures to mitigate harm to civilians, including the prevention of sexual and gender-based violence, protection of vulnerable groups, and strengthening early warning and response mechanisms.  1.4  Collaboration and Coordination: Collaboration and coordination among peacekeeping actors, including the United Nations, regional organizations, and host country authorities, are crucial for effective operations. Enhancing coordination mechanisms, information sharing, and joint planning can improve the overall effectiveness and efficiency of peacekeeping efforts.  1.5  Local Ownership and Engagement: Peacekeeping missions should prioritize local ownership and engagement throughout the process. This includes involving local communities, civil society organizations, and local authorities in decision-making, peace processes, and implementation of initiatives. Local actors have a deep understanding of the context and can contribute valuable insights and sustainable solutions.  1.6  Gender Mainstreaming: Integrating gender perspectives throughout all stages of peacekeeping operations is vital. Recognizing the specific needs, experiences, and contributions of women and promoting their meaningful participation in decision-making processes helps ensure more inclusive and effective  peace building  efforts.  1.7  Capacity Building and Institutional Development: Investing in capacity building and institutional development is crucial for sustainable peace. Future missions should prioritize building the capacities of local institutions, security forces, and civil society organizations. This includes training, mentoring, and providing technical assistance to develop robust governance structures, rule of law institutions, and security sector reforms.  1.8  Conflict Prevention and Early Warning: Early warning systems and conflict prevention strategies should be prioritized. Strengthening mechanisms for detecting and responding to early signs of tensions or conflict can help prevent the escalation of violence and reduce the need for more extensive peacekeeping interventions.  1.9  Strategic Communication and Public Information: Effective communication strategies that engage with local communities, disseminate accurate information, and counter misinformation are critical. Clear and transparent communication about the mission's objectives, activities, and progress builds trust and understanding among the local population.  1.10  Flexibility and Adaptability: Peacekeeping missions should be flexible and adaptable to changing circumstances on the ground. The ability to adjust strategies, approaches, and resource allocation based on evolving dynamics is essential to meet emerging challenges and effectively respond to the needs of the host country.        While there are valuable lessons learned from peacekeeping efforts in Mali, there are also potential challenges to implementing these lessons  that these work could identify.  One of the challenges in implementing lessons learned is the ongoing political instability in Mali. The recent coup in Mali in 2020, for example, has raised concerns about the country's ability to transition to democratic rule and addres s the root causes of conflict. Also, t he security situation in Mali remains precarious, with various armed groups operating in the region. Ensuring the safety and security of peacekeeping forces, humanitarian workers, and local populations is essential, and future peacekeeping efforts may f ace challenges in this regard.  Peacekeeping  operations require significant resources, including financial, human, and material resources. With competing   demands for resources, there may be challenges in securing adequate funding for  peacekeeping efforts in Mali. Without forgetting  Collaboration and coordination among various peacekeeping actors, including the United Nations, regional organizations, and host country authorities, are crucial for effective operations. However, achieving such coordination can be challenging, given the diverse interests and priorities of different actors.        Mali is a diverse country, with multiple ethnic groups and languages spoken. Ensuring effective communication and cultural sensitivity across diverse communities can be challenging for peacekeeping  forces and other stakeholders.  Building local capacity is crucial for sustainable peace. However, limited local capacity, particularly in terms of governance structures, security forces, and civil society organizations, can be a challenge in implementing lessons lear ned from peacekeeping efforts. And  integrating  gender perspectives throughout all stages of peacekeeping operations is essential. However, there may be challenges in addressing deep-seated gender inequalities and empowering women to participate in decision-making processes. There is much attention given to Peacekeeping activities which is referred to as an effective asset to maintain stability in an area, as many countries especially in Africa are conflict-driven .  But to reach this objective i.e. longlasting peace, w hen conducting peace operations, the UN need to  take into consideration several parameters on  operational challenges  especially on a non-permissive environment.  Even with the best intentions and strategies, there may be gaps in implementing lessons learned. Ensuring effective implementation of policies and strategies requires commitment, resources, and st rong leadership at all levels.  Finally, external factors, such as geopolitical interests, regional dynamics, and global economic trends, can also affect the success of  peacekeeping efforts in Mali though t hese factors may be beyond the control of peacekeeping forces and other stakeholders. - Успех миротворческих усилий в Мали.
Задачи:
- Рассмотреть факторы, влияющие на успех миротворческих миссий.
- Оценить роль международных сил в поддержании мира в Африке.
- Исследовать эффективность миротворческих операций Организации Объединенных Наций (ООН) в Африке.
- Анализировать вызовы и перспективы миротворчества в Африке.

Обратите внимание, что в данном тексте отсутствуют конкретные цели и задачи, направленные на решение определенной проблемы или улучшение ситуации в Мали. Однако можно выделить общую цель – успешное проведение миротворческих усилий в регионе, а также ряд задач, связанных с анализом факторов, влияющих на успех таких миссий, оценкой роли международных сил и исследованием эффективности миротворческих операций ООН. Также можно отметить вызовы и перспективы миротворчества в Африке, но без более конкретных рекомендаций или планов действий. В целом, анализ этого текста показывает, что он больше академического характера, предназначенный для обмена идеями и пониманием проблем миротворчества, нежели практического руководства по решению конкретных задач.
